item.content:encoded: Oh, this one was clever! It had me guessing the whole time until the end. It gave me a chuckle at the last iteration, and I truly enjoyed the read, from the initial confusion on what the hell was going on, to the end of the file when we get a proper explanation and learning that the Foundation is basically messing with Edmunson just to see how far this scip can go. Honestly, this speaks to the mischief maker inside me, and it definitely made my day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am very glad to hear you enjoyed it. Thank you for the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked it personally, it was a well-written joke that I didn't see coming. It still manages to be an interesting SCP in of-itself, without the humorous angle. Good job!

WHERE. ARE. THE. BEES.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate your thoughts. I did try to avoid a direct compulsion effect, in that nobody who is affected on this thing feels like they have to work on this thing, but they're assigned to work on this thing, and therefore, they're going to be subject to its effect. So any random person will accept there are bees in the room and not care, but Edmunson has been assigned to contain these bees, and since he accepts they're in there, everything he's doing is because it's his job, not because he "has" to do it. The room only carries the belief, not the action. I tried to illustrate this in the iterations, especially with testing the "sense of safety" contents twice and coming up with different results, which wouldn't necessarily happen in a compulsion effect.

This is why they can act a bit ridiculous, because it's ridiculous to hold the view that there are bees in that room, even though that's exactly what's happening. Watching people bend over backwards to avoid the dissonance in their beliefs is always a little funny, which is the core of this article. If Ayim didn't see bees in a room that clearly must have bees in it, perhaps it's because he forgot his reading glasses and therefore would struggle to see tiny things, since that's what reading glasses are for. Certainly a better explanation than there not being bees in the room.

I do appreciate that you liked the origin story. That was the part I was least sure about, so I'm glad to know it didn't mess things up. The idea for the blacked-out SCP actually came before this idea, and I wanted to have a "placeholder" if I ever decide to write up the 001 proposal that created this SCP room.

Thank you for your thorough feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Votes don't help the author, so I've vowed to always leave a comment with mine, at the very least whenever I rate an article negatively. If you feel the same, help out by leaving critique with more of your votes. Comment follows:

I have a couple of problems with this concept. I really don't like compulsion effects, and this is one of those articles that's a compulsion effect in a pretty wrapper. I find it a bit irritating when an article asserts that Foundation staff aren't intelligent enough to work these things out, and/or haven't already been warned that such anomalies exist. Some of the early tests - such as the problem being attributed to a researcher wearing the wrong glasses - accentuate this.

I also don't like how there's so much testing. In the first half of the article, it's justified, with research staff trying to work out what's going on when things don't add up (although if they scrapped their assumptions, they'd solve it pretty quickly). In the second half of the article, where I assume the researcher works out what's happening pretty quickly, much of the tests are pointless. I hold the stance that once the Foundation knows how to contain something, testing should stop.

One thing that I did really appreciate, though, was the nod to how the anomaly was created. So many articles, even those that describe anomalies affecting Foundation equipment, don't bother to try to explain where it came from. Your solution to that is quick but effective and I've not seen it before.

It's a -1 overall, though.

item.title: SCP-5765: +1
item.content:encoded: This wiki sure has a great history of working with bees. I wonder how these small, hard-to-find bees would react meeting those TALL BEES.
